words.shortDefinition: Spending disproportionate time on trivial decisions while ignoring important ones.
words.longDefinition: The tendency to focus excessive attention on minor, easy-to-grasp details (like the color of a bikeshed) while avoiding complex, critical decisions (like nuclear reactor design). A productivity anti-pattern in engineering teams.
words.techContext: Invoked when teams spend too long debating minor issues like code formatting or naming conventions instead of addressing architecture or performance concerns.
words.exampleSentence: We spent two hours bikeshedding the button color instead of discussing the database migration strategy.
words.etymology: from Parkinson's Law of Triviality (1957) — hypothetical committee debates bikeshed color endlessly.

words.shortDefinition: Improving code structure without changing its behavior.
words.longDefinition: The process of restructuring existing code to make it cleaner, more maintainable, or more efficient while preserving its external behavior. A key practice in maintaining healthy codebases.
words.techContext: Discussed when improving code quality, reducing technical debt, or preparing code for new features.
words.exampleSentence: Before adding the new feature, I spent a day refactoring the authentication module.
words.etymology: coined by Martin Fowler in 1999, combining "re-" (again) + "factoring" (breaking into components).

words.shortDefinition: Independent components that don't affect each other when changed.
words.longDefinition: In software architecture, refers to system components or design decisions that can be changed independently without impacting other parts. Borrowed from mathematics where orthogonal vectors are perpendicular.
words.techContext: Used when discussing modular design, separation of concerns, and clean architecture.
words.exampleSentence: Our authentication and authorization systems are orthogonal, so we can swap auth providers without touching permissions.

words.shortDefinition: An operation that produces the same result no matter how many times it runs.
words.longDefinition: A property of certain operations where applying them multiple times has the same effect as applying them once. Critical for safe retries in distributed systems and HTTP methods like PUT and DELETE.
words.techContext: Commonly used in API design and distributed systems discussions.
words.exampleSentence: The retry logic is safe because the endpoint is idempotent.
words.etymology: from Latin idem ("same") + potent ("power") — coined 1870 in algebra.
